reason: Consider evaluating the `time` crate for simpler datetime needs — not a mandate to replace `chrono`; chrono remains maintained and is often correct for timezone-heavy work
LazyLock is stable since Rust 1.80 and can remove an extra dependency after source imports are updated
LazyLock and OnceLock are stable since Rust 1.80 and may cover common once_cell usage after source imports are updated
structopt functionality was folded into clap derive; migrate when you are ready to update CLI derives and attributes
axum is a common Tokio-native choice for new services, but actix-web remains maintained; treat this as an ecosystem preference, not a mandatory migration
Iron is no longer a good foundation for new Rust web services; axum is a maintained Tokio-native migration target
memmap is unmaintained; memmap2 is the maintained fork, but source imports and API details still need review
failure is deprecated and unmaintained; anyhow for application errors and thiserror for library errors is the common modern split
tracing adds structured spans and async-aware instrumentation, but log is still maintained and may be enough for simple libraries
reqwest can deserialize JSON directly when its json feature is enabled; cargo-bless only suggests this when serde_json is not used directly in source
using two async runtimes usually adds complexity; consolidate only after checking runtime boundaries and dependencies
tracing-subscriber pairs with tracing for structured, filterable logging; keep env_logger if log-based output is intentionally simple
tracing plus tracing-subscriber is the modern observability stack, but migration requires source instrumentation choices
axum is the more active Tokio-native web framework choice for new projects; migration from warp should be deliberate
axum is a common lightweight Tokio-native choice, but Rocket may still be appropriate if its ergonomics are important to the project
std collection constructors cover many maplit use cases after source macros are rewritten
error-chain is in maintenance mode; anyhow for app errors and thiserror for library errors is the common modern split
proc-macro diagnostics need compile-time errors with spans; syn::Error is the standard baseline and proc-macro-error2 is an ergonomic option
derivative appears inactive; derive_more may cover many derive helper use cases, but macro behavior needs source review
error-chain and anyhow overlap; pick one error handling pattern based on whether this is an application or library boundary
bytes already provides the core zero-copy byte buffer primitives; verify the helper crate is not adding project-specific parsing behavior
available_parallelism covers common CPU count queries in std after source calls are updated
rustc-serialize is obsolete; serde is the standard serialization ecosystem, but migration is a real source change
serde exposes derive through its own feature; this can usually remove the separate serde_derive dependency without source rewrites
some crossbeam-utils use cases are now covered by std, while parking_lot may fit some locking needs; inspect actual APIs before changing
axum can reduce boilerplate for application HTTP JSON handling, but hyper remains right for lower-level HTTP code
clap exposes derive through its own feature; this can usually remove the separate clap_derive dependency without source rewrites
quick-error is unmaintained since 2018 — anyhow (app) + thiserror (lib) is the modern standard
failure_derive was part of the deprecated failure crate — thiserror provides better derive macros

replacement: no replacement needed (modern Rust handles nested macros)
reason: proc-macro-nested was a workaround for old macro limitations — no longer needed in modern Rust
version_check is a legacy crate for MSRV detection — use package.rust-version field instead
semver crate is rarely needed directly — use cargo_metadata for dependency resolution
A simpler option for 2d games only.
serde_cbor is officially abandoned — its README says 'This project is no longer maintained. Please use ciborium instead.' ciborium is the maintained RustCrypto replacement with a compatible API.
serde_yaml was archived by its author (dtolnay) in 2024; serde_yml is the community-maintained fork with a compatible API and active releases.
tempdir is deprecated — its crates.io page says 'Moved to tempfile::TempDir'. Last release was 2018; tempfile provides TempDir with the same API.
std::io::IsTerminal was stabilized in Rust 1.70 and covers virtually all atty use cases; most crates have already migrated and atty is unmaintained.
docopt is effectively unmaintained (no meaningful releases since 2021) and the Rust ecosystem has standardized on clap for CLI argument parsing.
slog is an older structured logging framework; tracing is the modern ecosystem choice with async-aware spans, compatible subscribers, and wider crate adoption.
ansi_term was unmaintained for years; owo-colors is a zero-dependency, no-std-compatible alternative with an ergonomic API. nu-ansi-term is another maintained fork.
the term crate is old and rarely updated; termcolor is the BurntSushi-maintained replacement with cross-platform ANSI and Windows console support.
futures-preview was the pre-stabilization async crate — async/await has been stable since Rust 1.39; use the futures crate directly.
async fn in traits is stable since Rust 1.75 — async-trait is no longer needed for non-dyn trait usage. Note: if you use 'dyn Trait' with async methods, async-trait may still be required.
chan has had no releases since 2017 and is effectively abandoned; crossbeam-channel is the maintained, high-performance drop-in with a compatible API.
log4rs is a log-based framework; tracing plus tracing-subscriber is the modern structured observability stack with async-aware spans and wider ecosystem support.
simple_logger is a minimal log facade; tracing + tracing-subscriber is the modern Rust observability stack with structured fields, async-aware spans, and a richer subscriber ecosystem.
openssl requires a native C library (libssl) and is a frequent source of CVEs; rustls is a pure-Rust TLS stack with no native dependencies and a strong security track record.
termion is Unix-only and not actively maintained; crossterm is a cross-platform terminal manipulation library that works on Unix and Windows and is actively maintained.
getopts is an old C-style argument parser that has had no meaningful updates in years; clap v4 with the derive feature provides ergonomic, type-safe CLI argument parsing with rich help generation.
fern is a runtime-configurable log framework; tracing + tracing-subscriber is the modern ecosystem choice with async-aware spans, structured events, and broad crate compatibility.
